title: The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change: An Empirical Exploration
study_design_description: The paper proposes and tests a model of how computerization of workplace tasks alters job content and human skill demands. The analysis exploits two sources of variation: changes over time in occupational employment distribution (extensive margin), and changes in skill content within occupations between 1977 and 1991 using matched occupations from the Dictionary of Occupational Titles (intensive margin). The study uses non-institutionalized employed workers ages 18–64, with individual observations aggregated to 140 consistent Census industries spanning all sectors of the economy for 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, and 1998.
primary_task_description: Jobs are conceptualized as a series of tasks: moving an object, executing a calculation, communicating information, resolving a discrepancy. The analysis focuses on computerization of manual tasks and information processing (cognitive) tasks. Non-routine manual activities require visual and manual skills; routine tasks are those sufficiently well-specified or performed in controlled environments that they can be automated.
population_description: Non-institutionalized employed workers ages 18–64. Individual worker observations are aggregated to 140 consistent Census industries spanning all sectors of the economy. All analyses use as weights full-time equivalent hours of labor supply, calculated as the product of Census or CPS sampling weight times hours of work in the sample reference week and, for Census samples, weeks of work in the previous year.

outcomes.outcome_description: Direction, Control, and Planning (DCP) of activities; captures interactive and managerial skills
GED-MATH; quantitative skills ranging from arithmetic to advanced mathematics required in occupations
STS (adaptability to work requiring Set limits, Tolerances, or Standards)
FINGDEX (Finger Dexterity); indicator of routine manual activity
EYEHAND (Eye-Hand-Foot coordination); measure of non-routine motor tasks
Share of employment accounted for by college graduates
Log relative demand for college-educated labor

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: rapidly computerizing industries vs other industries
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Industries intensive in non-routine cognitive tasks and intensive in routine cognitive and routine motor tasks in 1959 computerized significantly more than other industries over the subsequent four decades.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: rapidly computerizing industries vs other industries
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Conversely, industries intensive in non-routine manual tasks computerized substantially less than others.
The proportion of the labor force employed in occupations that made intensive use of non-routine cognitive tasks – both interactive and analytic – increased substantially.
In contrast, the percentage of the labor force employed in occupations intensive in routine cognitive, routine manual and non-routine manual activities declined over the period.
while both measures of non-routine cognitive tasks trended upward during the 1960s, the upward trend in each accelerated substantially thereafter, and was most rapid during the 1980s and 1990s.
Equally notably, routine cognitive and routine manual tasks were both increasing during the 1960s, prior to commencing a decline in the 1970s, which became more rapid in each subsequent decade.

For the non-routine cognitive/interactive task measure, the growth in economy-wide input of this task over four decades is almost entirely a within-industry phenomenon; moreover, the rate of within-industry growth accelerates from decade to decade.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: rapidly computerizing industries vs other industries, 1980s-1990s
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Industries that computerized relatively rapidly during the 1980s and 1990s increased the share of jobs requiring non-routine cognitive/analytic task input during these decades more than did other industries. However, this was not the case for prior decades.

the estimated impact of computerization on both measures of non-routine cognitive tasks accounts for eighty to one hundred percent of the observed within-industry growth in input of these tasks over 1980 – 1998.

Beginning in the 1970s, these same sectors undertook rapid reductions in routine cognitive and routine manual task input. As above, the impact of computerization accounts for all of the observed within-industry reduction in routine task input between 1970 and 1990.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: rapidly computerizing industries vs other industries
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Consistent with our framework, there is no significant evidence that rapidly computerizing industries decreased their use of non-routine manual tasks more rapidly than did other industries during this period.
Computerization is estimated to have increased the college graduate employment share by 5.0 percentage points over 1980 – 1998 and 5.3 percentage points over 1970 – 1998.
For reference, the estimate of 5.0 percentage points can be compared to the actual change in college graduate employment between 1980 and 1998, which is 6.83 percentage points.

For the period 1980 – 1998, task shifts induced by computerization are estimated to have increased log relative college demand by 2.8 log points annually (Table 10, Panel C). Comparing this number to the estimated economy-wide shift of 6.7 log points over this period, we find that changes in task content explain approximately 40 percent of the observed shift.
The analogous calculation for the three-decade interval finds that computer-induced task shifts explain approximately 30 percent of the demand shift favoring college graduates over 1970 – 1998.

notes: This is an observational study using historical data from the Dictionary of Occupational Titles (DOT), Census IPUMS samples, and Current Population Survey (CPS) data. No randomized arms or experimental treatment/control groups are present. The paper develops a theoretical framework predicting that computers are more substitutable for routine tasks than non-routine tasks, and tests this framework using industry-level and occupation-level variation in computerization and task content. The DOT has known limitations including limited sampling of occupations (particularly in service sector), imprecise definitions, and omission of important job skills. Measured changes along the intensive margin (within-occupation task content) are likely conservative estimates. The weighted fraction of employment reevaluated between 1978 and 1990 was 73%, with occupations chosen for reevaluation partly on expectation that content had changed, potentially biasing estimates. Measurement error in DOT measures is likely to bias estimates towards zero. The paper notes that relative demand for college-educated labor increased substantially from the 1940s through 1960s prior to computerization, so computerization's contribution must be interpreted as acceleration beyond pre-existing trends.
